5 1 Hydrodynamics 1.1 Introductory remarks Fluid dynamics is one of the most central branches of astrophysics. It is essential to understand star formation, galactic dynamics (what is the origin of spiral structure?), accretion discs, supernovae explosions, cosmological ’¼éows, stellar structure (what is inside the Sun?), planet atmospheres, the interstellar medium, and the list could go on.
Fluids such as water can usually be considered incompressible, be-cause extremely high pressures of the order of thousands of atmospheres are required to achieve appreciable compressions. Air is highly compress-ible, but it can behave as an incompressible ’¼éuid if the ’¼éow speed is much smaller than the sound speed. Astrophysical ’¼éuids, on the other hand, must usually be treated as compressible ’¼éuids. This means that we must account for the possibility of large density changes.
Astrophysical ’¼éuids usually consists of gases that are ultimately made of particles. Although they are not exactly continuous ’¼éuids, most of the time they can be treated as if they are. This approximation is valid if the mean free path of a particle is small compared to the typical length over which macroscopic quantities such as the density vary. If this is the case, one can consider ’¼éuid elements that are i) large enough that are much bigger than the mean free path and contain a vast number of atoms ii) small enough that have uniquely de’¼üned values for quantities such as density, velocity, pressure, etc.
E’¼Ćects such as viscosity and thermal conduction are a consequence of ’¼ünite mean free paths, and extra terms can be included in the equations to take them into account in the continuous approximation.
Most astrophysical ’¼éuids are magnetised. Although this can some-times be neglected, there are many instances in which it is necessary to take explicitly into account the magnetised nature of astrophysical ’¼éuids.
Therefore we shall study magnetohydrodynamics alongside hydrodynam-ics.
1.2 The state of a ’¼éuid In the simplest case, the state of a ’¼éuid at a certain time is fully speci’¼üed by its density Žü(x) and velocity ’¼üeld v(x). # [Basic, learning examples of applications of CFD and MHD in astrophysics, and current research directions using these techniques?](/questions/44194/basic-learning-examples-of-applications-of-cfd-and-mhd-in-astrophysics-and-cur)

I am an undergraduate in Physics, and I have an opportunity to work with a some of my seniors to learn Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) and Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD).

I would like to choose my first individual project in order to learn about how these techniques are used in Astrophysics.

## 2 Answers 2

Computational Fluid Dynamics and Magnetohydrodynamics are very important in understanding the interior of stars, star formation, the interstellar medium, accretion discs around stars,... So to answer your first question, I would say this is a great opportunity and it would be very beneficial to learn about it through a hands-on project with more experienced people.

![usernumber's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/07a9daf52292249017711175df2acea1?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG&f=y&so-version=2)

I am an undergraduate in Physics, and I have an opportunity to work with a some of my seniors to learn Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) and Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD).

Congrats!

I would like to choose my first individual project in order to learn about how these techniques are used in Astrophysics.

The answer to your question depends on what you mean here. I assume you mean that you want to do an exercise, when you say "individual project." You want a topic within these fields that are well understood - that is, you are not interested in **currently open questions/problems**.

Further, since you're an undergraduate, you likely have not studied classical electrodynamics at an advanced level (perhaps you have?), which adds a difficulty in even understanding CFD/MHD exercises.

A big part of CFD/MHD is coding. It's very simulation heavy, unless you're doing pure theory, but I do not suspect that you intend to advance theory (as discussed above).

So, you could try to

In terms of finding an "exercise," [this](https://arxiv.org/abs/1301.5572) article/book by Spruit might be too advanced, perhaps your senior fellows can help you find an appropriate exercise, but there are many (see chapter 3). [Here](https://wwwmpa.mpa-garching.mpg.de/%7Ehenk/mhd12.pdf)'s the most recent version of it. Since you're an undergrad, I recommend just doing choosing a problem in electrodynamics, e.g., from Griffith's textbook, since a lot of MHD/CFD problems are applications of Maxwell's laws, etc... See [here](https://dragonfly.tam.cornell.edu/teaching/mae5230-cfd-intro-notes.pdf) for some standard methods in CFD.

enriched_points.point_title: Specific examples of fluid dynamics in action
enriched_points.expanded_content: Astrophysical fluid dynamics manifests in phenomena like stellar winds, accretion disks, and the interstellar medium, showcasing complex behaviors of gases and plasmas influenced by gravity.
Recent studies have revealed insights into magnetohydrodynamic instabilities in supernovae and the dynamics of accretion flows, enhancing our understanding of cosmic structures and processes.

Understanding fluid dynamics informs theories of star formation, galaxy evolution, and cosmic structure, providing critical insights into the behavior of matter under extreme astrophysical conditions.
